skill.name: automate-whatsapp
skill.description: Build and operate WhatsApp automations in Kapso by managing workflow graphs, triggers, executions, functions, app integrations, and database records.
skill.summary: Create, update, debug, and deploy Kapso WhatsApp automation workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: AI review found no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection in SKILL.md. Most static findings are false positives caused by Markdown command examples, but the skill has medium operational risk because it directs use of Kapso API credentials and scripts that can change WhatsApp workflows, functions, integrations, triggers, and database rows.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Kapso API Credential Use
medium_findings.description: The skill requires KAPSO_API_BASE_URL and KAPSO_API_KEY, then guides use of scripts that operate against Kapso services. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it exposes sensitive credentials to agent-driven workflows and should be used only in trusted workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential names and scripted workflow operations are explicit in SKILL.md. I found no evidence of exfiltration, so the risk is privileged use rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Scripts Can Modify External Automation State
medium_findings.description: The documented scripts can create, update, deploy, invoke, and delete workflow resources, triggers, functions, integrations, and database rows. A mistaken or compromised prompt could cause unwanted changes in a connected Kapso account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script table clearly lists mutating operations and notes some blocked operations. The finding is medium because this appears to be intended administration functionality, not hidden behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command References Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The numerous external command detections are inline Markdown examples and script names in SKILL.md, not Ruby backtick execution. They still indicate that users may run local Node scripts, but the static Ruby-specific pattern is not semantically correct here.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation containing backticked commands and table entries. No Ruby code or shell interpolation appears in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Source Metadata
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL detection points to the source repository URL in frontmatter. This is metadata, not a runtime network destination or data exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 4 is a source field that identifies the upstream skill location. I found no instruction on that line to send data to the URL.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches Only
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections align with ordinary documentation text such as the description, graph edge rules, and asset table. No cryptographic operation is present in the reviewed SKILL.md content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or password handling. The scanner appears to have matched substrings in normal prose.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Detections Are Expected Inventory Commands
low_findings.description: The list and search operations enumerate Kapso workflows, phone numbers, accounts, actions, tables, and executions. This inventory behavior is expected for an automation administration skill, but users should scope API keys carefully.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected commands are named list or search operations in documented workflows. They can expose account metadata, but their purpose is consistent with the skill description.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Directed External Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to run local Node scripts. These commands can affect external Kapso resources when credentials are configured.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly use node scripts for mutating and inspection tasks. The risk depends on the script implementations and credential scope.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Based API Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on environment variables for Kapso host and API key configuration. These secrets should not be exposed in prompts, logs, or shared workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The required environment variables are directly documented. No evidence shows misuse, but the presence of an API key is a meaningful security factor.

content.value_statement: WhatsApp automation work often requires careful workflow, trigger, function, and database changes. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for Kapso automation tasks.
actual_capabilities: Guides workflow graph fetch, validation, editing, and update steps.
Documents trigger management for inbound WhatsApp events.
Supports execution inspection through context values and event history.
Explains function creation, deployment, retrieval, and invocation workflows.
Covers app integration discovery, account selection, and tool attachment.
Documents database table lookup and row create, update, upsert, query, and delete tasks.
limitations: Requires a configured Kapso API base URL and API key.
Does not provide WhatsApp onboarding or template approval guidance.
Variable create and delete operations are documented as unsupported by the Platform API.
Raw SQL execution is not supported through the documented Platform API path.

use_cases.title: Launch a support intake flow
use_cases.description: Create a WhatsApp workflow that receives inbound messages, gathers customer details, and routes issues to the correct team.

use_cases.title: Maintain workflow logic safely
use_cases.description: Fetch a workflow graph, preserve node identifiers, validate changes, and update the graph with lock version protection.

use_cases.title: Debug failed conversations
use_cases.description: Inspect executions, read context values, review events, and identify where a WhatsApp automation stopped or waited.

prompt_templates.title: List Available Workflows
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the automate-whatsapp skill to list my Kapso workflows and summarize which ones appear related to WhatsApp automation.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Inbound Trigger
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the automate-whatsapp skill to find my WhatsApp phone number ID and create an inbound_message trigger for workflow <workflow_id>.

prompt_templates.title: Update a Workflow Graph
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the automate-whatsapp skill to fetch workflow <workflow_id>, add a decision branch for VIP customers, validate the graph, and update it with the correct lock version.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy an Integrated Agent Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the automate-whatsapp skill to add an agent node with an app integration, deploy a helper function, and verify the updated workflow through execution inspection.

output_examples.input: List workflows and identify the active WhatsApp automation.
output_examples.output: A short workflow inventory with names, identifiers, active status, and a recommendation for the workflow to inspect next.

output_examples.input: Debug execution ex_123 for missing customer data.
output_examples.output: A concise execution report showing current status, relevant context values, recent events, and the likely node that needs correction.

output_examples.input: Add a WhatsApp trigger for an existing workflow.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step action summary confirming the selected phone number, trigger type, trigger identifier, and activation state.

best_practices: Use least-privilege Kapso API keys and rotate them after shared debugging sessions.
Always fetch the latest graph and lock version before applying workflow changes.
Validate graph files locally before updating a production workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not paste Kapso API keys into prompts, chat logs, or workflow definitions.
Do not change existing node IDs when editing a workflow graph.
Do not run mutating scripts against production before reviewing the target workflow and payload.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates Kapso workflow operations for WhatsApp conversations, including graphs, triggers, executions, functions, integrations, and database rows.

faq.question: Does it send WhatsApp messages directly?
faq.answer: It focuses on Kapso workflow automation. Direct WhatsApp onboarding, templates, and messaging are handled by related skills.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: The documented setup requires KAPSO_API_BASE_URL and KAPSO_API_KEY environment variables.

faq.question: Can it edit workflow graphs?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes how to fetch, validate, update, and re-fetch workflow graph definitions.

faq.question: Can it deploy custom functions?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents function creation, deployment, retrieval, invocation, and invocation history workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe for production accounts?
faq.answer: Use it with caution. The skill can guide mutating operations, so review targets and use scoped credentials.
